skill.name: testing-with-sandbox
skill.description: Guides safe testing of outbound email with Mailtrap Email Sandbox, including sandbox API endpoints, SMTP settings, inbox IDs, tokens, and common mistakes.
skill.summary: Test outbound email safely with Mailtrap Sandbox inboxes before production delivery.
skill.author: mailtrap

security_audit.summary: Static command-execution, weak-crypto, and reconnaissance alerts are false positives caused by Markdown inline code, headings, and documented URLs. The confirmed risk is legitimate network and email-sending guidance for Mailtrap Sandbox API and SMTP, which requires user credentials and should be published with a warning.

medium_findings.title: Legitimate Network and Email Sending Guidance
medium_findings.description: The skill documents Mailtrap Sandbox API and SMTP use, including bearer tokens, sandbox hostnames, inbox IDs, and message endpoints. This is expected for the skill purpose, but misuse could send test data to an external Mailtrap service or expose credentials in prompts or logs.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network behavior is explicitly documented and central to the skill. The risk is operational rather than malicious because the endpoints are Mailtrap sandbox endpoints and the content warns against production use.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Inline Code Treated as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The external command alerts point to Markdown backticks around related skill names, variables, hostnames, and endpoint examples. No executable Ruby, shell script, or command runner is present in the skill file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are Markdown documentation, not source code. They contain inline code formatting only, with no interpreter context or execution path.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptographic Algorithm Alert
low_findings.description: The weak-crypto alerts occur in descriptive Markdown and documentation links, not in cryptographic code. No hashing, encryption, or signature implementation is present in the reviewed skill file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines contain frontmatter text and a documentation URL. There is no cryptographic API call or algorithm selection to evaluate.

low_findings.title: False Positive: System Reconnaissance Alert
low_findings.description: The reconnaissance alerts refer to documentation about Mailtrap account IDs and sandbox host selection. The skill does not instruct system enumeration, local host probing, or unauthorized discovery.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines describe normal Mailtrap account and endpoint selection for authorized API use. No local or third-party reconnaissance behavior appears in context.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Hardcoded Official URLs
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL alerts are documentation links and Mailtrap API endpoint examples. They do not hide traffic destinations or point to unrelated third-party collection services.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs align with the skill publisher and documented Mailtrap products. They are visible to users and are not embedded in executable code.

content.user_title: Test Email Delivery with Mailtrap Sandbox
content.value_statement: Email tests can accidentally reach real recipients or miss delivery defects. This skill helps route mail to Mailtrap Sandbox, inspect captured messages, and separate test credentials from production sending.
actual_capabilities: Explains when to use Mailtrap Email Sandbox instead of live email delivery.
Identifies Sandbox API and SMTP endpoint patterns for test inboxes.
Guides token, account ID, and inbox ID selection for sandbox workflows.
Summarizes common sandbox testing mistakes and their fixes.
Points users to Mailtrap SDKs and documentation for current integration details.
limitations: Does not provide full framework-specific setup tutorials.
Requires valid Mailtrap sandbox credentials supplied by the user.
Does not deliver email to real recipients from the sandbox environment.
Does not replace current Mailtrap API documentation for exact field details.

use_cases.title: Capture Staging Email
use_cases.description: Route staging application mail to a Mailtrap test inbox before a release.

use_cases.title: Validate Message Content
use_cases.description: Inspect headers, HTML, attachments, and spam indicators in captured messages.

use_cases.title: Automate Email Assertions
use_cases.description: Use sandbox message endpoints to confirm that expected transactional emails were sent.

prompt_templates.title: Choose Sandbox Setup
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner planning a safe email test environment.
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me decide whether to use Mailtrap Sandbox SMTP or the Sandbox API for my development app.

prompt_templates.title: Configure Test Credentials
prompt_templates.scenario: Developer separating sandbox and production credentials.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my planned Mailtrap Sandbox environment variables and explain how to avoid using live sending tokens.

prompt_templates.title: Design Email QA Checks
prompt_templates.scenario: QA team creating repeatable message inspections.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a checklist for validating captured Mailtrap Sandbox emails, including headers, HTML, attachments, and spam score.

prompt_templates.title: Plan CI Assertions
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced automation using the Sandbox API.
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a CI workflow that sends a test email to Mailtrap Sandbox, fetches the captured message, and verifies expected content.

output_examples.input: I need to test password reset emails without sending them to customers.
output_examples.output: Use Mailtrap Sandbox for the staging environment, set sandbox SMTP credentials, and verify each captured reset message in the test inbox.

output_examples.input: Our test suite must confirm that an invoice email was generated.
output_examples.output: Use the Sandbox API to list recent messages for the inbox, find the invoice email, and assert the subject, recipient, and key body text.

output_examples.input: We are unsure which Mailtrap token to use for sandbox testing.
output_examples.output: Use a token with testing or sandbox scope only, store it separately, and avoid reusing the live sending token.

best_practices: Keep sandbox tokens and live sending tokens in separate environment variables.
Confirm the inbox ID before running automated tests against captured messages.
Check current Mailtrap SDK documentation before generating integration code.
anti_patterns: Do not expect sandbox messages to reach real recipients.
Do not reuse production API tokens for sandbox testing.
Do not hardcode sandbox credentials into source files or prompts.

faq.question: Does this skill send production email?
faq.answer: No. It focuses on Mailtrap Email Sandbox, where messages are captured for testing.

faq.question: Can it help with SMTP testing?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains when to use sandbox SMTP settings for apps that already send via SMTP.

faq.question: Can it help with API-based tests?
faq.answer: Yes. It describes Sandbox API endpoints for sending, listing, and fetching test messages.

faq.question: Does it include every framework setup?
faq.answer: No. It points users to Mailtrap documentation and SDK README files for current framework details.

faq.question: What credentials are required?
faq.answer: Users need Mailtrap sandbox credentials or a token with testing or sandbox scope.

faq.question: Is it safe for CI workflows?
faq.answer: Yes, when sandbox credentials are stored securely and tests use a dedicated test inbox.
